questionText: Two pipes A and B can fill a tank in 18 hrs and 6 hrs respectively. If both the pipes are opened simultaneously, how much time will be taken to fill the tank?

steps.explanation: Part of tank filled by A in 1 hour
Part of tank filled by B in 1 hour
Time = reciprocal of combined rate = 9/2 hrs

solution.commonMistake: Adding times directly instead of the rates

questionText: Two pipes can fill a tank in 12 hours and 16 hours respectively. A third pipe can empty the tank in 30 hours. If all three pipes are opened and function simultaneously, in how much time will the tank be full? (in hours)

steps.explanation: Part filled by first pipe in 1 hour
Part filled by second pipe in 1 hour
Part emptied by third pipe in 1 hour

solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to subtract the emptying pipe's rate

modelName: Three pipes two fill one drain

questionText: Pipes A and B together fill a tank in 6 hours. A alone takes $\frac{2}{3}$ of the time B alone takes. Find how long A alone takes to fill the tank.

steps.explanation: Let B alone take x hrs; A alone takes 2x/3 hrs. Combined: 3/(2x)+1/x=1/6
Both statements independently give A=10 hrs (answer c)

solution.commonMistake: Confusing which variable represents A vs B

questionText: Pipes A and B together fill a tank in 6 hours. A alone takes x hours and B alone takes (x+5) hours. Find how long A alone takes.

solution.commonMistake: Accepting negative root x=-3

questionText: Pipe A fills a tank 3 times as fast as pipe B. Together A and B fill in some time. B alone takes 60 min. How long does B take to fill $\frac{1}{3}$ of the tank?

steps.explanation: From solution: 40/x=2/3 → x=60. B alone takes 60 min
Time for B to fill 1/3 of tank

solution.shortcut: B alone=60 min; 1/3 of tank=20 min
solution.commonMistake: Finding time for full tank instead of 1/3

questionText: A pipe fills a cistern in 9 hours. Due to a leak at the bottom it takes 10 hours to fill. In what time will the leak alone empty the cistern?

solution.commonMistake: Subtracting times directly instead of rates

questionText: Pipe A fills a tank in 16 hours. A's work rate is 150% of pipe B's rate. Find how long A and B together take to fill the tank.

steps.explanation: A fills in 16 hrs. 1/16=(3/2)(1/B) → B=24 hrs

solution.commonMistake: Misinterpreting 150% -- A is faster not slower

questionText: Pipe A fills a tank in 4 hours and pipe B in 6 hours. How long do A and B together take to fill the tank?

solution.commonMistake: Converting 0.4 hrs to 40 min instead of 24 min

questionText: Pipe P fills a tank at 15 litres/min (Statement I). Tank capacity is 5400 litres (Statement III). Which statements are needed to find how long P takes to fill the tank?

steps.explanation: From I and III: time=5400/(15*60)=6 hrs. Statement II not needed.

solution.shortcut: Volume/rate=time; only need capacity (III) and rate (I)
solution.commonMistake: Assuming all three statements are necessary

questionText: An inlet pipe fills a tank at $x$ litres/min and an outlet pipe empties at $y$ litres/min ($x>y$). Tank capacity is $T$ litres. How long does it take to fill the tank?

solution.shortcut: Time = Total capacity / Net fill rate
solution.commonMistake: Using x+y instead of x-y

questionText: Pipes A (fills in 20 hrs), B (fills in 30 hrs), C (empties in 40 hrs) are all opened. Find net part filled per hour.

steps.explanation: LCM(20,30,40)=120; A fills 6 units/hr, B 4 units/hr, C drains 3 units/hr

solution.commonMistake: Adding all rates without subtracting emptying rate

questionText: Three pipes: pipe 1 fills in 3 hrs, pipe 2 empties in 7 hrs, pipe 3 fills in 10 hrs. All open. How long to fill the tank?

steps.explanation: Net rate: 1/3-1/7+1/10. From solution: net=19/210 per hour

solution.shortcut: Net rate=19/210; time=210/19 hrs approx 11 hrs
solution.commonMistake: Getting signs wrong for fill vs empty pipes

questionText: Three pipes have diameters 1 cm, $\frac{4}{3}$ cm, and 2 cm. Filling time is inversely proportional to square of diameter. The 2 cm pipe fills in 61 min. How long do all three together take?

solution.commonMistake: Using diameter directly instead of squaring it

questionText: One tap fills a tank in 6 hrs. After 3 hrs (half filled), 4 taps are opened together. How long does the remaining half take? Total time?

steps.explanation: One tap fills in 6 hrs; in 3 hrs fills half tank
Time for 4 taps to fill remaining 1/2: (1/2)/(2/3)=3/4 hr=45 min

solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to add the initial 3 hours

questionText: A cistern can be filled by a tap in 5 hours and emptied by another in 8 hours. Both opened simultaneously. How long to fill the cistern?

solution.commonMistake: Subtracting wrong way; must use fill rate minus drain rate

questionText: Two filling pipes and one drain pipe open on a half-full tank. Net emptying rate is 1/9 per hr. How long to empty the tank?

steps.explanation: Net part emptied per hour=1/9 (from solution)
Time to empty 1/2 of tank at 1/9 per hr

solution.commonMistake: Using full tank instead of half-full

questionText: Two pipes fill a tank in 50 min and 60 min. A third pipe empties it in 75 min. All three opened together. How long to fill the tank?

solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to subtract the emptying pipe

questionText: 8 pipes fitted: each fill pipe fills in 12 hrs, each waste pipe empties in 36 hrs. All 8 open; tank fills in 3 hrs. How many waste pipes?

solution.commonMistake: Setting up equation with wrong signs

questionText: A pipe fills a tank in 3 hrs. Due to a leak it takes $3\frac{1}{2}$ hrs to fill. If full, how long does the leak take to empty the tank?

steps.explanation: Fill rate=1/3; net rate with leak=2/7

solution.commonMistake: Subtracting times instead of rates

questionText: Pipe A fills in 5 hrs, B in 20 hrs. Both open; due to a leak it takes $\frac{9}{2}$ hrs to fill. When full, how long does the leak take to empty the tank?

solution.commonMistake: Not combining A and B before computing leak rate

questionText: Pipes A, B, C fill a tank in 30, 20, 10 min discharging solutions P, Q, R. All opened for 3 min. What is the proportion of solution R in the tank after 3 min?

solution.commonMistake: Using total tank capacity instead of volume filled in 3 min

questionText: Two pipes A and B together fill a cistern in 4 hours. B would take 6 hours more than A alone. How long does A alone take?

solution.commonMistake: Not rejecting negative root

questionText: A faster pipe fills 3 times as fast as a slower pipe. Together they fill in 3 min. How long does the slower pipe alone take?

solution.commonMistake: Incorrect ratio setup between the two pipes

questionText: Pipe A alone takes x hrs. B takes x/2 hrs and C takes x/4 hrs. Together all fill in 5 hrs. Find x.

solution.commonMistake: Inverting the rates of B and C

questionText: First pipe takes x hrs, second (x-5) hrs, third (x-9) hrs. First and second together have same rate as third alone. Find x.

solution.commonMistake: Accepting x=3 which makes x-9 negative

questionText: A tank is filled by a tap in 12 min at 13.5 litres/min. Each bucket holds 9 litres. How many buckets are needed to fill the tank?

solution.commonMistake: Confusing capacity with rate

questionText: Bucket P holds x litres, Q holds x/3 litres. Drum holds 60x litres. Using both P and Q per turn, how many turns to fill the drum?

solution.commonMistake: Not adding P and Q capacities correctly

questionText: Pipes A and B fill a tank in 12 and 15 min. Both opened; after 3 min A turned off. How long does B take to fill the rest?

steps.explanation: Part filled by A+B in 3 min: 3*(1/12+1/15)=3*9/60=9/20

solution.commonMistake: Not closing A after exactly 3 minutes

modelName: Pipe closed partway find remaining time

questionText: Pipes A and B fill a tank in 15 and 20 min. Both opened together; after 4 min A is shut. When will the tank be full?

solution.shortcut: After 4 min: 8/15 remains; B: 32/3 min; total=14 min 40 sec
solution.commonMistake: Not converting fractional minutes to seconds

modelName: Pipe A shut after 4 min total time

questionText: Pipes A, B, C fill/drain a tank in 15, 20, 25 hrs (C drains). All open for 10 hrs then C closed. In how many more hours will A+B fill the tank?

steps.explanation: Net rate A+B-C: LCM=300; 20+15-12=23; net=23/300 per hr

solution.shortcut: After 10 hrs: 7/30 remains; A+B rate=7/60; time=2 hrs
solution.commonMistake: Including C's contribution after it is closed

questionText: Pipes A and B fill a tank in 60 and 40 min. A alone used for half the time; A+B together for the other half. Find total time to fill the tank.

solution.commonMistake: Wrong formula for the split-time phase

modelName: A alone half time A+B other half

questionText: Pipes A and B fill a cistern in 12 and 15 min. Both opened; after 5 min pipe C (emptying) also opened. Cistern full at 10 min total. How long for C alone to empty it?

steps.explanation: Net empty rate when all 3 open: 1/6-1/12-1/15... solution says 1/60. C alone empties in 60 min from answer key (d)

solution.commonMistake: Confusing net rate with drain rate

modelName: Two fill pipes plus drain find drain time

questionText: Pipes A and B fill a tank in 20 and 30 hrs. A leakage empties 1/3 of the water filled. With the leak, how long for A+B to fill the tank?

steps.explanation: Leak empties 1/3 of what is filled. Extra time to refill 1/3: 12*1/3=4 hrs

solution.shortcut: A+B=12 hrs + 4 hrs to refill 1/3 leaked=16 hrs
solution.commonMistake: Treating leak as a constant rate

questionText: Four pipes fill a reservoir in 15, 20, 30, 60 hrs. First pipe opened at 8 a.m., second at 9 a.m., third at 10 a.m., fourth at 11 a.m. When will reservoir be full?

steps.explanation: Let full t hrs after 8 a.m. Pipe 1 works t hrs, 2 works (t-1), 3 works (t-2), 4 works (t-3)

solution.commonMistake: Not accounting for staggered start times

questionText: Full tank: drain pipe 1 empties in 12 hrs (8 a.m.), fill pipe fills in 15 hrs (9 a.m.), drain pipe 2 empties in 4 hrs (11 a.m.). When is the tank empty?

steps.explanation: Tank empty t hrs after 8 a.m. From solution: 4t+5(t-1)-15(t-3)=0 → -6t+40=0 → t=20/3

solution.commonMistake: Getting signs wrong for fill vs drain pipes

questionText: A pipe fills at 1/4 per hr; a leak drains at 1/24 per hr. In 2 hrs depth rises 0.5 m. What is the full depth?

steps.explanation: Net fraction filled in 2 hrs (solution gives 5/24): (5/24)*h=0.5

solution.commonMistake: Using gross fill rate ignoring leak

modelName: Pipe with leak find tank depth

questionText: Pipes A and B fill a tank in 6 and 4 hrs alternately (A for 1 hr, B for 1 hr, etc.). How long to fill the tank?

steps.explanation: Remaining=1/6; A's turn; A fills 1/6 in 1 hr; total=5 hrs

solution.shortcut: 2-hr cycle=5/12; 4 hrs=5/6; 1/6 left; A fills in 1 hr; total=5 hrs
solution.commonMistake: Not checking whose turn it is for the final fraction

questionText: Pipe A fills in 12 hrs, B in 15 hrs, C in 20 hrs. A+B work for 1 hr, then A+C for 1 hr, alternately. How long to fill the tank?

steps.explanation: 7th hr: A+B rate=3/20; fills exactly in 1 hr. Total=7 hrs

solution.shortcut: 2-hr cycle=17/60; 6 hrs=17/20; 3/20 remains; A+B fills in 1 hr; total=7 hrs
solution.commonMistake: Not tracking which pair is active in 7th hour

questionText: Rotation: A alone 1 min, B alone 1 min, C alone 1 min. A fills in 10 min, B in 15 min, C empties in 20 min. How long to fill the tank?

solution.shortcut: 3-min cycle=7/60; 8 cycles=14/15; A fills 1/15 in 2/3 min; total=24 2/3
solution.commonMistake: Applying wrong pipe's rate for final fractional time

questionText: A pump fills a tank; empties it 10 m3/min faster. Emptying takes 8 min more than filling. Capacity=2400 m3. Find fill capacity.

solution.commonMistake: Setting up time difference with wrong sign

questionText: Inlet alone fills in 8 hrs. With outlet open, fills in 12 hrs. Tank capacity=2400 L. What is the inlet's flow rate in litres per minute?

solution.commonMistake: Using net rate instead of inlet-only rate

questionText: Two inlet pipes fill a tank in 15 and 20 min. A waste pipe empties it. All three open; the waste pipe's empty time is to be found (answer from solution and answer key).

steps.explanation: Waste pipe empties in 24 min (answer per key (c); net rate with waste=(7/60-1/24))

solution.commonMistake: Using net fill rate as waste rate

questionText: Pipes A and B fill a tank in 75 and 45 min. Both open; after x min B turned off; A fills remaining. Total time=30 min. Find x.

steps.explanation: A+B for x min then A alone for (30-x). Total=1

solution.commonMistake: Wrong setup of A's contribution over full 30 min

questionText: Pipes fill in 16 and 12 min. Obstruction reduces rates to 7/8 and 5/6 of normal. After x min obstruction removed; 3 more min at full flow fills the tank. Find x.

steps.explanation: Full rate=1/16+1/12=7/48; part in 3 min at full=3*7/48=7/16
Remaining to fill at reduced rate: 1-7/16=9/16. From solution: x=9/2=4.5 min

solution.commonMistake: Not applying obstruction reduction to each pipe separately

questionText: Three pipes take 5, 10, and 15 hrs to fill a tank. Opened one at a time with fixed gap x hrs. Total fill time=5 hrs. Find x.

steps.explanation: First pipe=5 hrs, second=(5-x) hrs, third=(5-2x) hrs. Fill eq: 5/5+(5-x)/10+(5-2x)/15=1

solution.commonMistake: Mixing hours and minutes in the equation
